front: Film: opens with a razor slicing a woman's eyeball, intercut with a cloud crossing the moon; ants crawl from a man's hand
back: Un Chien Andalou (Luis Buñuel & Salvador Dalí)

front: Film: a man drags two pianos containing decaying donkeys (and two priests) across a room
back: Un Chien Andalou (Luis Buñuel & Salvador Dalí)

front: Film: inspired the Pixies song 'Debaser' — 'slicing up eyeballs, I am un chien andalusia'; title changed from '-ou' to '-usia' because it sounded 'too French'; opens Doolittle
back: Un Chien Andalou (Luis Buñuel & Salvador Dalí)

front: Film: Buñuel created the Liebestod/Argentine tango sound design by hiding behind the screen alternating two records; described in his autobiography My Last Sigh
back: Un Chien Andalou (Luis Buñuel & Salvador Dalí)

front: Film: David Bowie screened this film instead of hiring an opening act for his 1976 Station to Station tour
back: Un Chien Andalou (Luis Buñuel & Salvador Dalí)

front: Film: opens with documentary footage of scorpions; lovers Gaston Modot and Lya Lys are repeatedly sexually thwarted by bourgeois French society
Film: a woman erotically sucks the toes of a religious statue; caused a riot when far-right Frenchmen destroyed paintings shown alongside it
Film: bourgeois friends repeatedly attempt to dine together but are constantly interrupted; walls of the set fall as characters realize they are being watched by an audience
back: The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie (Luis Buñuel)
work: The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie (1972)

front: Film: recurring shot of six people walking silently down a country road (Buñuel said 'Then it must be good' when told it appeared in ten other films)
back: The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie (Luis Buñuel)
work: The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie (1972)

front: Film: Catherine Deneuve plays a sexually repressed housewife who secretly works as a prostitute in the afternoons; recurring horse hooves and bells symbolize her desires
Film: Alfred Hitchcock complimented the director on the scene revealing the heroine's leg amputation, saying 'ah, that leg, that leg'
Film: Friedkin's casting agent accidentally cast Fernando Rey (from this film) as the villain of The French Connection
Film: bourgeois dinner guests inexplicably cannot leave the music room; inspired partly by Sartre's No Exit; characters smash a wall to reach a water pipe
Film: would-be nun turns her uncle's estate into a home for beggars; a leper plays Handel's 'Hallelujah Chorus,' leading to a Last Supper-style photo
Film: Francisco falls in love with Gloria at a foot-washing ceremony; later stands in a bell tower contemplating strangling his wife
Film: follows children in a Mexico City slum; Pedro's body is thrown onto a pile of garbage at the end
back: Los Olvidados / The Young and the Damned (Luis Buñuel)
work: Los Olvidados (The Young and the Damned, 1950)

front: Director: Spanish surrealist; collaborated with Salvador Dalí on Un Chien Andalou and L'Age d'Or; autobiography titled My Last Sigh
